record.name: Senolytics coverage repair assessment
record.summary: The senolytics track has an auditable vertical slice across preclinical D+Q discovery/healthspan evidence, early human biomarker and pilot human studies, a phase 2 randomized bone trial, a 2025 cognition/mobility pilot, translation-review context, risk-of-bias records, and registry-only active trial-watch records. D+Q bone safety extraction now includes registry aggregates, full-text publication-table preferred-term rows beyond headache, and registry/article discontinuation fields. DKD, IPF, and AD-risk records now cite PubMed EFetch source snapshots for metadata-only abstract-level sample, dosing, timepoint, effect, and safety details, with the legacy IPF tolerability result reconciled to the N=12 randomized pilot; those snapshots are not full-text/table sources. Registry surveillance remains incomplete because ClinicalTrials.gov snapshots for deferred fisetin frailty registries could not be fetched in the worker, and the review landscape remains incomplete because no systematic-review or meta-analysis source was screened. Full article/table safety and efficacy extraction for DKD, IPF, AD-risk, and D+Q bone supplement material remains incomplete.

evidence_categories.rationale: Includes early D+Q human biomarker, randomized IPF pilot, phase 2 bone, and AD-risk cognition/mobility pilot records; DKD, IPF, and AD-risk now have metadata-only PubMed EFetch snapshots supporting abstract-located extraction for sample, dosing, timing, or effect details.
Includes foundational discovery work plus the old-mouse healthspan and post-treatment survival paper.
Cellular senescence targeting is anchored by the discovery source and translation review, but pathway-level extraction is not yet complete.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Future extraction should separate senescent-cell survival pathways, SASP markers, and tissue-specific clearance evidence.

evidence_categories.rationale: The IPF, bone, and AD-risk records explicitly encode mixed, pilot, primary/subgroup, and feasibility limitations instead of flattening them into positive claims; the AD-risk record now includes the abstract-level MoCA and TNF-alpha estimates.

Safety-oriented records now include the D+Q bone registry aggregate counts, full-text adverse-event table extraction, structured preferred-term rows for headache, diarrhea, nausea, constipation, gastrointestinal reflux, vertigo, fatigue, and body pain, and registry/article discontinuation fields. IPF and AD-risk safety slices now cite PubMed EFetch source snapshots for abstract-level safety statements, and DKD now has a metadata-only source-snapshot-backed abstract limitation record. Cross-human safety remains thin because DKD, IPF, and AD-risk still need full article/table safety extraction and harmonized event definitions.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Run full article/table extraction for DKD, IPF, and AD-risk safety details, reconcile D+Q bone supplement safety material if available, and keep cross-human safety synthesis blocked until term definitions, denominators, and relatedness/seriousness fields are harmonized.

payload_summary.abstract_excerpt: Senescent cells, which can release factors that cause inflammation and dysfunction, the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP), accumulate with ageing and at etiological sites in multiple chronic diseases. Senolytics, including the combination of Dasatinib and Quercetin (D + Q), selectively eliminate senescent cells by transiently disabling pro-survival networks that defend them against their own apoptotic environment. In the first clinical trial of senolytics, D + Q improved physical function in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), a fatal senescence-associated disease, but to date, no peer-reviewed study has directly demonstrated that senolytics decrease senescent cells in humans. payload_summary.abstract_excerpt: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is an age-related, chronic, irreversible fibrotic lung disease. IPF is associated with increased senescent cells burden, which may be alleviated with administration of senescent cell targeting drugs termed 'senolytics'. We previously conducted an open-label single-arm pilot study of the senolytic combination of dasatinib and quercetin (D + Q) in patients with IPF but lack of control group limited interpretation and next-stage trial planning. The primary objective of this confirmatory randomized placebo-controlled pilot trial (RCT; NCT02874989) was to report adverse events with D + Q and inform study feasibility for future efficacy trials. payload_summary.abstract_excerpt: This single-arm study evaluates the feasibility, safety, and preliminary effects of two senolytic agents, Dasatinib and Quercetin (DQ), in older adults at risk of Alzheimer's disease. Participants took 100 mg of Dasatinib and 1250 mg of Quercetin for two days every two weeks over 12 weeks. Recruitment rate, adverse events, absolute changes in functional outcomes, and percent changes in biomarkers were calculated. Spearman correlations between functional and biomarker outcomes were performed. Approximately 10% of telephone-screened individuals completed the intervention (n = 12). There were no serious adverse events related to the intervention. Mean Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) scores non-significantly increased following DQ by 1.0 point (95% CI: -0.7, 2.7), but increased significantly by 2.0 points (95% CI: 0.1, 4.0) in those with lowest baseline MoCA scores.
